external_indicators.detail: US foreign aid contraction directly threatens LMIC consulting demand. USAID priority geographies overlap with Acasus footprint (Pakistan, Afghanistan, sub-Saharan Africa).
UK aid budget frozen at 0.5% GNI (down from 0.7%). FCDO is a likely Acasus funder given UK registration and LMIC focus.
Requires rare combination: field experience in LMICs + data/analytics skills + consulting pedigree. 22% headcount growth YoY signals hiring pressure.

external_indicators.indicator: Big 4 / MBB entry into impact consulting
external_indicators.detail: McKinsey, BCG, Deloitte all expanding social impact practices. Compete for same government and multilateral contracts.
Growing market overall, though Acasus LMIC niche is a small slice dependent on donor funding cycles.

external_indicators.indicator: AI readiness for data-driven reform delivery
external_indicators.detail: Budapest data engineering team (24 people) with AWS, Tableau, ETL pipelines. Positioned to absorb AI tooling for government analytics.

internal_indicators.value: Active
internal_indicators.detail: Founder Fenton Whelan stepped to Chair in 2025. New CEO Jonny Barty (internal promote) still establishing. Transition during growth phase adds execution risk.

internal_indicators.value: Undisclosed but likely moderate
internal_indicators.detail: Aid-funded consulting = lumpy contracts. Loss of 1-2 major government programs could materially impact $35M revenue base.
Below average. Field deployment in conflict/post-conflict zones drives burnout risk. Could affect retention of scarce talent.

internal_indicators.value: +22% YoY
internal_indicators.detail: Strong growth signal. Expanding from ~217 to ~265 staff in 12 months. Confirms pipeline health.

external_indicators.value: 49,577 students in 2025, up from 43,979 in 2008 (+12.7% over 17 years, CAGR 0.71%). Accelerating post-2021: 2.5% growth in 2022, 1.8% in 2024, 1.3% in 2025.
external_indicators.detail: ACARA data shows a clear growth acceleration from 2021 onwards after a flat/declining period 2015-2019. Westmead Catholic Community turning away 500+ enrolment applicants annually (web-research.json) confirms excess demand. Western Sydney population growth is the structural driver — diocese covers a high-growth corridor from Parramatta to Blue Mountains.

external_indicators.indicator: Unmet enrolment demand in Westmead precinct
external_indicators.value: 500+ applicants turned away annually at Westmead Catholic schools
external_indicators.detail: Structural excess demand in the Westmead corridor demonstrates market strength. The merger of Sacred Heart Primary and Mother Teresa Primary into a new four-stream K-6 campus at 2 Darcy Road (opening Term 3, 2026) is a direct capacity response. An early learning centre is also planned for 2027 on the same site.

external_indicators.indicator: LBOTE student proportion across NSW Catholic schools
external_indicators.value: 36% of NSW Catholic school students are Language Background Other Than English (LBOTE)
external_indicators.detail: Western Sydney has significantly higher LBOTE concentrations than the NSW average. This creates both opportunity (community trust in Catholic schools as inclusive institutions) and complexity (differentiated support demands, EAL/D resourcing, interpreter services). No CSPD-specific LBOTE breakdown available in source files.

external_indicators.indicator: NSW teacher shortage — systemic sector-wide headwind
external_indicators.value: Teacher recruitment and retention named as central theme at Sydney Morning Herald Schools Summit 2024. Inter-Diocesan Teacher Workforce Working Group met five times in 2024.
external_indicators.detail: The teacher shortage is not CSPD-specific — it is a NSW-wide and national structural issue. CSNSW's 2024 annual report identifies teacher workforce sustainability as a sector challenge. CSPD's response includes a Teacher Scholarship Program (102 inaugural recipients in 2024, $5,000/year tuition + laptop) targeting Year 12 students for the teaching pipeline. This is a proactive but long-horizon solution — supply relief is 4-6 years away from scholarship recipients entering classrooms.
external_indicators.source: csnsw-annual-report-2024.pdf p.30 & p.73 (via annual-report-extract.json); cspd-annual-report-2023.pdf p.26 (via annual-report-extract.json)

external_indicators.indicator: Religious Education accreditation requirement adds hiring friction
external_indicators.value: Primary classroom and RE secondary teachers must hold RE accreditation (undergraduate 6 units or postgraduate 4 units) in addition to standard NESA accreditation and WWCC
external_indicators.detail: The dual-accreditation requirement for Catholic school teachers narrows the eligible candidate pool relative to public and independent school competitors, amplifying the NSW-wide teacher shortage. In a tight market, candidates with RE accreditation command a premium or may choose more accessible pathways.

external_indicators.value: NSW Catholic Schools System received approximately $3.65 billion in recurrent government funding (Commonwealth + State) in 2024. New Needs-Based Funding Distribution Model 2024-2029 approved July 2023, implemented January 2024.
external_indicators.detail: The five-year funding arrangement provides planning certainty through 2029, supporting CSPD's five-year strategic plan. Commonwealth recurrent grants are the dominant revenue source for CSPD at 63.8% of total operating revenue ($546.8m of $857.2m in 2023). Loadings add approximately 24% on top of base funding.

external_indicators.indicator: Better and Fairer Schools Agreement (2025-2035) unresolved
external_indicators.value: NSW Government had not concluded negotiations with Commonwealth at end of 2024. NSRA extended a further year for NSW. Negotiations continuing in 2025.
external_indicators.detail: The unresolved BFSA creates medium-term funding uncertainty for the period after the current 2024-2029 arrangement. CSNSW launched 'Keep School Funding Fair' campaign in December 2024, citing research showing lack of public understanding leaves the non-government funding model vulnerable ahead of elections. Any renegotiation that reduces Catholic school funding relativities would materially impact CSPD given its 80%+ government revenue dependency.

external_indicators.indicator: Online assessment cost-recovery transition from July 2025
external_indicators.value: NSW Department of Education will shift access to online assessments (including Year 1 Phonics Screening Check) to cost-recovery from July 2025 for non-government schools
external_indicators.detail: Creates a new per-assessment cost burden that did not previously exist. For a system the size of CSPD (49,577 students), this could be material. CSNSW is actively advocating for equitable access. Affects CSPD's ability to participate in voluntary diagnostic assessments that support curriculum reform implementation.

external_indicators.indicator: NSW-wide mobile phone ban in schools
external_indicators.value: Australia's under-16 social media ban enacted December 2025. NSW government direction aligning with this trend. CSPD enacted system-wide ban from start of 2026.
external_indicators.detail: The regulatory environment has shifted firmly toward school phone restrictions. CSPD's early adoption (announced November 2025, effective January 2026) positions it ahead of the curve. Most CSPD schools already had informal restrictions. The policy aligns with CSPD's 'domed' AI/device approach. Neutral externally because the requirement is sector-wide — not a competitive differentiator.
external_indicators.source: web-research.json (regulatory: NSW-wide mobile phone restrictions; initiatives: Mobile Phone Ban)

external_indicators.indicator: NSW Curriculum Reform — mandatory explicit teaching rollout 2020-2027
external_indicators.value: First comprehensive NSW curriculum reform in three decades. Schools implementing revised syllabuses K-6, 7-10, and 11-12 simultaneously. Implementation period 2020-2027.
external_indicators.detail: Scale and simultaneity of reform creates significant professional development and compliance burden. CSPD's Uplift framework (launched August 2025) is explicitly aligned to the NSW explicit teaching mandate — this is a strategic bet that the reform direction is durable. If the reform stalls or reverses, CSPD's pedagogical investment may face headwinds. Risk of uneven implementation quality across 89 schools.
external_indicators.source: csnsw-annual-report-2024.pdf p.59 & p.72 (via annual-report-extract.json); web-research.json (regulatory: NSW Curriculum Reform)

external_indicators.value: Rising complexity: notable increase in support requests for student violence, self-harm, and problematic sexualised behaviour. CSNSW also managing historical abuse claims.
external_indicators.detail: The Royal Commission into Institutional Responses to Child Sexual Abuse created enduring compliance obligations for Catholic schools. CSNSW data shows rising frequency and complexity of child protection cases in 2024. CSPD established a Wellbeing Directorate (2022) with 140+ specialist teachers, social workers and psychologists — indicating structural investment in this area. Liability exposure from historical claims remains a reputational and financial risk.
external_indicators.source: csnsw-annual-report-2024.pdf p.63-64 (via annual-report-extract.json); cspd-annual-report-2023.pdf p.33-35 (via annual-report-extract.json)

external_indicators.indicator: Violent extremism threat escalation in education sector
external_indicators.value: ASIO escalated national terrorism threat level from 'possible' to 'probable' in August 2024. Substantial increase in reported incidents across education sector. CSNSW secured $2.8m NSW Government CVE funding through 2028.
external_indicators.detail: As a visibly Catholic institution in a diverse, multicultural Western Sydney community, CSPD has non-trivial exposure to extremism-adjacent risks. CSNSW funding to end of 2028 provides resourcing but the threat level elevation suggests sustained vigilance requirements with ongoing operational cost.

external_indicators.indicator: NSW public school building program — competitive pressure
external_indicators.value: NSW Government infrastructure investment in public schools is accelerating alongside population growth in Western Sydney. No specific spend figures available in source files.
external_indicators.detail: Evidence is indirect: CSPD's 10-Year Capital Framework explicitly identifies 'growth' and 'rising enrolment' as investment drivers, and the Westmead demand overflow indicates Catholic schools are already capacity-constrained. A well-resourced public school building program in the same growth corridors (Schofields, Jordan Springs, Box Hill) could absorb new residents before CSPD can expand capacity. Data on specific NSW public school builds in the diocese was not available in source files.

external_indicators.indicator: NAPLAN reforms — shift from grades to proficiency levels
external_indicators.value: NAPLAN proficiency standards framework already in use at CSPD. 2023 NAPLAN data drove CSPD strategy: shifting students from 'Strong' to 'Exceeding', particularly in Numeracy.
external_indicators.detail: NAPLAN reform to proficiency reporting raises public visibility of performance gaps. CSPD's 2023 annual report explicitly acknowledges a numeracy ceiling (insufficient students reaching 'Exceeding'). This is now an externally visible indicator. Schools with lower proficiency concentrations may face reputational headwinds if media coverage highlights Catholic school aggregate performance vs public or selective school benchmarks.

external_indicators.indicator: AI governance in schools — 'domed' access model
external_indicators.value: CSPD policy: students access only CSPD-approved AI platforms. Open generative AI (e.g. ChatGPT) is blocked system-wide.
external_indicators.detail: The 'domed' approach is a defensible governance posture given safeguarding obligations and the age of the student cohort. However, as AI tools become more deeply embedded in tertiary and vocational education, a highly restrictive school-level approach may disadvantage students' AI literacy development. The June 2025 LIFTED Student Leaders Forum explicitly addressed AI — suggesting CSPD is aware of this tension. Balance between protection and preparation is an evolving challenge.
external_indicators.source: web-research.json (technology: CSPD AI policy; initiatives: LIFTED Forum)

internal_indicators.indicator: Patrick Ellis hire — EGM Learning Outcomes
internal_indicators.value: Confirmed as EGM Learning Outcomes, role recruited via ANZUK Executive with deadline September 12, 2025. Previously Education Lead at Catholic Education Canberra-Goulburn (CECG).
internal_indicators.detail: Ellis's LinkedIn post references 'high quality, evidence based teaching and learning #Uplift' — directly associating the new hire with the Uplift framework. His 20 years across Parramatta, Wilcannia-Forbes, and Canberra-Goulburn provides system-level literacy and Catholic education network depth. Returning to Parramatta after CECG experience brings cross-system benchmarking capability. This is a senior appointment aligned to CSPD's primary strategic bet on evidence-based teaching.

internal_indicators.value: System-wide teaching and learning framework launched August 2025. Covers all 80 schools, 43,000+ students. Endorsed by AERO CEO Dr Jenny Donovan at 2025 Ann D Clark Lecture.
internal_indicators.detail: Uplift operationalises explicit instruction, formative assessment, and structured literacy — methodologies with strong AERO/NESA backing. External endorsement from AERO's inaugural CEO gives the framework credibility. The framework explicitly moves away from open-plan classroom designs, which signals a clear pedagogical stance. Implementation consistency across 89 schools is the execution risk. No independent outcome data yet available — framework is too new.
internal_indicators.source: web-research.json (initiatives: Uplift); annual-report-extract.json (uplift_details note on timing)

internal_indicators.value: Announced October 4, 2024. Six pillars: Mission, People, Quality, Relevance, Sustainability, Trust.
internal_indicators.detail: Strategy was developed through consultation with 80 school principals, staff, and families. Jack de Groot described it as 'detailed, earnest and ambitious.' The 2023 annual report noted the need for an 'organisation-wide strategy' to be crystallised in 2024 — this plan fulfils that commitment. The Sustainability pillar explicitly addresses financial stewardship and scalable systems, which is relevant given the 2023 operating deficit.
internal_indicators.source: web-research.json (initiatives: Five-Year Strategic Plan); annual-report-extract.json (raw_quotes p.4)

internal_indicators.value: New three-storey K-6 campus at 2 Darcy Road, Westmead. Merger of two existing primary schools. Four-stream capacity. Term 3, 2026 opening.
internal_indicators.detail: Directly addresses documented unmet demand (500+ annual applicants turned away). The consolidated four-stream school replaces two under-capacity schools and is co-located with Catherine McAuley Westmead and Parramatta Marist High School, creating a K-12 precinct. Ambrose Early Learning Centre planned for 2027 on the same site extends the pipeline. Execution risk: construction and transition management across two merging school communities.

internal_indicators.value: Canvas (Instructure) deployed across all CSPD schools. Accessible any device. Features: grading, announcements, discussions, parent visibility. Dedicated CSPD Canvas support team.
internal_indicators.detail: A standardised LMS across 89 schools enables consistent data capture, reduces per-school IT overhead, and creates a platform for system-wide professional learning (Canvas Catalog). Parental visibility feature supports the community engagement pillar of the strategic plan. The existence of a legacy system (Classm8 referenced alongside Canvas) suggests a transition period with dual-platform risk.

internal_indicators.value: NSW and ACT Catholic Systemic Schools Enterprise Agreement 2025. Approved by 99% of CSPD employees (vote closed October 31, 2025). Teacher pay: 3% from Oct 2024, 3% Oct 2025, 3% Oct 2026. General Employees: 4% from Jul 2024, 3% from Jul 2025, 3% from Jul 2026.
internal_indicators.detail: 99% approval is an unusually strong mandate — signals strong workforce relations and union-management alignment. The EA includes improved parental leave, superannuation, three days ceremonial leave for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander employees, and a CPI adjustment clause if inflation exceeds 4.5% in 2026. The three-year certainty aids workforce planning but increases known salary cost commitments against an already operating-deficit background (2023: -$20.6m).

internal_indicators.value: -$20.6m deficit from operations before capital (total operating revenue $857.2m vs expenditure $877.9m). Salaries and wages = $628.4m (71.6% of expenditure). Capital outflows $85.9m.
internal_indicators.detail: The operating deficit is structural — salary costs dominate and are now locked in at +3% annually under the EA 2025. Capital expenditure ($85.9m in 2023) is funded partly by the Diocesan School Building Fund ($23.1m net) and capital grants ($12.2m), but net capital outflows ($85.9m) far exceed capital revenue ($36.2m). Revenue composition is 80.7% government-sourced (Commonwealth + State) making CSPD highly sensitive to funding policy shifts. The 2024-2029 FDM provides certainty but the structural deficit requires the Sustainability pillar of the strategic plan to deliver material efficiency gains.

internal_indicators.value: 69% employee engagement score in 2023. Survey response rate: 53%.
internal_indicators.detail: A 69% engagement score with only 53% response rate leaves meaningful uncertainty — non-respondents may skew negative. Staff feedback themes identified in the 2023 annual report: need for greater System Leader visibility, better communication, work-life balance, and wellbeing. The EA 2025 99% approval and multiple wellbeing initiatives since 2023 may have improved this, but no more recent data is available in source files.

internal_indicators.value: Ban effective January 2026 across all 80 schools. Most schools had informal restrictions already. Exceptions for medical necessity.
internal_indicators.detail: Proactive policy alignment with the Australian social media ban (under-16s) and NSW government direction. Reduces in-class distraction consistent with Uplift framework's evidence-based teaching focus. Announces a clear, consistent position that differentiates CSPD from less decisive competitors. Low implementation friction as most schools already had informal norms.
internal_indicators.source: web-research.json (initiatives: Mobile Phone Ban; regulatory: NSW-wide mobile phone restrictions)

internal_indicators.indicator: New governance model — company limited by guarantee
internal_indicators.value: CSPD became an incorporated company limited by guarantee from 1 January 2023. New Board replacing Diocesan Schools Council. Three Board committees: Audit and Risk; Mission, Learning and People; Infrastructure and Property.
internal_indicators.detail: Corporate governance model provides clearer accountability structures, cleaner financial reporting obligations, and greater operational independence from the Diocese compared to the previous Diocesan Schools Council structure. Board composition includes commercial, legal, financial, and educational expertise. The change was completed in 2023 — the organisation is now operating within the new model and the 2024-2029 strategy reflects the new governance regime.
internal_indicators.source: annual-report-extract.json (cspd strategic_priorities: New governance model p.3 & p.12)

external_risks.label: Government funding policy shift post-2029
external_risks.reasoning: 80.7% of CSPD operating revenue (2023) is government-sourced (63.8% Commonwealth, 16.9% State). The Better and Fairer Schools Agreement for 2025-2035 remained unresolved at end of 2024, with NSW being the holdout jurisdiction. CSNSW launched a 'Keep School Funding Fair' campaign citing public misunderstanding of the non-government funding model — indicating the sector itself perceives political vulnerability. Any federal or state government that shifts the funding formula would have outsized impact on CSPD relative to more fee-dependent independent schools. Score reflects high impact but partially mitigated by the 2024-2029 FDM already locked in.

external_risks.label: NSW teacher shortage deepening
external_risks.reasoning: Structural teacher shortage is evidenced by: Inter-Diocesan Workforce Working Group meeting frequency (5 times in 2024), naming at SMH Schools Summit, and CSPD's own pipeline-building response (Teacher Scholarship Program, 102 recipients). RE accreditation requirement further narrows CSPD's eligible candidate pool versus public and independent schools. CSPD hired 190 new teachers in 2023 — at this scale, even a modest deterioration in supply creates significant class coverage risk. Score reflects systemic severity with limited near-term mitigation available.

external_risks.label: NSW public school infrastructure expansion in growth corridors
external_risks.reasoning: Western Sydney population growth generates demand for all school sectors simultaneously. If NSW Government fast-tracks public school builds in high-growth suburbs where CSPD is capacity-constrained (e.g. Schofields, Jordan Springs, Box Hill), Catholic school market share could erode before CSPD's 10-Year Capital Framework delivers new capacity. Evidence is indirect — the Westmead demand overflow (500+ annual rejections) and capital grant activity in Schofields confirm growth pressure. No direct evidence of specific public builds competing with CSPD in source files; score is medium accordingly.

external_risks.label: Child safeguarding liability — historical and emerging
external_risks.reasoning: CSNSW data shows a notable increase in support requests for student violence, self-harm, and problematic sexualised behaviour in 2024. CSNSW is also managing historical abuse claims. For a Catholic system, post-Royal Commission scrutiny is structurally elevated and reputational risk from any individual school incident cascades to the whole system. CSPD's Wellbeing Directorate investment (140+ specialists) is a mitigant but does not eliminate liability exposure. The EA 2025 also introduced enhanced obligations on staff. Score reflects ongoing probability in a sector with elevated regulatory scrutiny.

external_risks.label: Online assessment cost-recovery transition
external_risks.reasoning: From July 2025, access to NSW DoE online assessments shifts to cost-recovery for non-government schools. For CSPD's scale this creates new direct costs. However, CSNSW is actively advocating for equitable access and this is a sector-wide issue (not CSPD-specific). The financial impact depends on utilisation rates across 89 schools. CSNSW has a track record of negotiating favourable terms. Score is low-medium; elevated only because the change directly intersects with CSPD's curriculum reform implementation priority.

external_risks.label: Inflation exceeding EA cost-of-living trigger in 2026
external_risks.reasoning: The EA 2025 contains a cost-of-living adjustment clause if inflation exceeds 4.5% in 2026. If triggered, salary costs would increase beyond the planned 3% annual increment, further widening the operating deficit. Current Australian inflation trajectory (as of early 2026) does not strongly indicate a breach of 4.5%, but the clause creates a contingent liability that must be monitored. Score reflects low probability but meaningful financial impact given the existing deficit position.

external_risks.label: NAPLAN proficiency visibility creating reputational pressure
external_risks.reasoning: CSPD's 2023 annual report explicitly acknowledges a numeracy gap (insufficient students at 'Exceeding' level). Under the NAPLAN proficiency reporting framework, this is now publicly visible. Media coverage that aggregates Catholic systemic school performance versus selective or independent school benchmarks could create community-level perception challenges in Western Sydney, where families increasingly have strong aspirations for academic outcomes. Parramatta Marist's 44th state ranking in HSC 2025 shows ceiling performance is possible — but system-wide proficiency uplift is a multi-year challenge.

internal_risks.label: Structural operating deficit with rising committed salary costs
internal_risks.reasoning: 2023 operating deficit of -$20.6m is structural: salaries ($628.4m, 71.6% of expenditure) are the dominant cost driver and are now locked in at +3% annually (teachers) and +3-4% (general staff) under the EA 2025. Capital expenditure ($85.9m in 2023) also significantly exceeds capital revenue ($36.2m). The Westmead campus and other 10-Year Capital Framework projects will sustain high capital outflows through 2026-2027. Revenue growth depends on enrolment growth (constrained by capacity) and government funding (5-year certainty but no real uplift). The Sustainability pillar of the 2024-2029 strategy must deliver meaningful efficiency gains to avoid deficit accumulation.

internal_risks.label: Uplift framework implementation consistency across 89 schools
internal_risks.reasoning: Launching a system-wide pedagogical framework across 89 schools with 3,760 teaching staff is a significant change management challenge. The Uplift framework was launched August 2025 — too recent for outcome data to be available. Patrick Ellis's hire as EGM Learning Outcomes signals CSPD recognises this execution risk. Quality of implementation will vary across schools depending on principal capability, staff engagement, and available professional development time. The 2023 employee engagement score (69%) and staff communication gaps noted in that report suggest the organisation has had prior challenges with system-wide change adoption.

internal_risks.label: Revenue concentration — 80% government-sourced
internal_risks.reasoning: Commonwealth recurrent grants alone represent 63.8% of operating revenue. Parent fees contribute only 14.6% ($125.5m). This concentration makes CSPD highly sensitive to any federal funding policy change. The BFSA negotiation uncertainty (see ext-risk-1) compounds this. CSPD's fee-waiver commitment for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander families (from 2026) adds a modest incremental revenue reduction. Unlike independent schools that can absorb government funding changes through fee adjustments, CSPD's systemic Catholic school model limits fee flexibility.

internal_risks.label: Westmead merger transition — two-school community consolidation
internal_risks.reasoning: Merging Sacred Heart Primary (Ralph Street) and Mother Teresa Primary into a single campus involves consolidating two distinct school communities, parent bodies, staff cultures, and traditions. Community-facing transitions at this scale carry relationship risk — parent dissatisfaction, staff displacement anxiety, or loss of local parish identity. The new campus opens Term 3, 2026 with Julianne Regan appointed as principal. No evidence in source files of community opposition or staff issues; risk is inherent to the merger structure rather than evidenced.

internal_risks.label: Staff engagement below sector norms
internal_risks.reasoning: 2023 employee engagement score of 69% with a 53% response rate is below what would be expected for a mission-driven organisation. The low response rate creates interpretation risk — non-respondents may be systematically more disengaged. Staff feedback themes (leadership visibility, communication gaps, work-life balance) suggest systemic issues rather than isolated concerns. The EA 2025's 99% approval and multiple subsequent wellbeing initiatives are positive signals, but the most recent engagement data available is 2023. If the score has not improved materially, it creates retention risk compounding the external teacher shortage.

internal_risks.label: Dual LMS period during Canvas transition
internal_risks.reasoning: Classm8 is referenced alongside Canvas in source materials, suggesting a transition period where both platforms may be in use across different schools. Dual-platform periods create staff training burden, data fragmentation, inconsistent parent experience, and support overhead. Risk is low because Canvas deployment appears substantially complete (described as 'system-wide' in web-research.json), but the Classm8 reference suggests the transition may not be fully resolved.

news_items.headline: Detailed, earnest and ambitious: Catholic Schools announces new strategy
news_items.summary: CSPD launched its 2024-2029 five-year strategic plan at Rooty Hill, with all 80 school principals attending. CEO Jack de Groot described the plan as 'detailed, earnest and ambitious.' Six strategic pillars: Mission, People, Quality, Relevance, Sustainability, Trust.

news_items.headline: Parramatta schools announce new five-year strategy
news_items.summary: CathNews coverage of the October 2024 strategy launch. Strategy aims for excellence, inclusivity, and Catholic tradition across 80 Western Sydney and Blue Mountains schools.

news_items.headline: CSPD Launches Innovate Reconciliation Action Plan 2025-2027
news_items.summary: CSPD launched its second Reconciliation Action Plan (Innovate level) at The Colebee Centre, Doonside. Artwork created by Amy Allerton in collaboration with 90 First Nations students and staff. Includes free school fees for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander families from 2026.

news_items.headline: The move to evidence-based teaching in our Catholic schools: Annual Ann D Clark Lecture inspires teachers
news_items.summary: 2025 Ann D Clark Lecture in Penrith. AERO CEO Dr Jenny Donovan endorsed CSPD's Uplift framework. Head of Primary Learning Lisa Crampton announced the new Effective Learning and Teaching Model. Explicit instruction and structured literacy are central to both.

news_items.headline: Western Sydney Catholic Schools and St Vincent de Paul Society NSW serving our communities
news_items.summary: CSPD became the first school-based education provider in NSW to formalise a partnership with St Vincent de Paul Society NSW. Partnership covers Vinnies Van volunteering, Mini Vinnies, Winter and Christmas appeals, School Sleepouts, and a service medallion for secondary students.

news_items.headline: Catholic systemic schools EA: 99% YES vote at CSPD
news_items.summary: IEU reports 98% average approval across nine NSW/ACT Catholic dioceses for new enterprise agreement. CSPD achieved 99% approval. Pay rises of 3% annually 2024-2026 for teachers. Enhanced parental leave, superannuation, and ceremonial leave for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander employees.

news_items.headline: Western Sydney Catholic Schools hold the phone
news_items.summary: CSPD announces system-wide mobile phone ban effective January 2026 across all 80 schools, aligned with Australia's social media ban for under-16s (December 2025). CEO Jack de Groot: 'Consistent restrictions with clear parameters are necessary to support both students and staff.'

news_items.headline: Four Western Sydney Catholic School students top the state in HSC
news_items.summary: Four CSPD students achieved HSC First-in-Course results. 20 NSW All Rounders system-wide. Parramatta Marist ranked 44th in NSW (29.3% Band 6) — highest-ranked Catholic systemic school in the state.

external_indicators.detail: 20.23% CAGR. Young population (median age ~25), underserved provincial demand, government subsidies driving growth.
Commission on Higher Education controls degree-granting authority, program approval, and tuition increase caps. Compliance cost but also barrier to entry.
Direct competitor in Philippine affordable HE. iPeople 74,000 enrollment is less than half PHINMA. Differentiated by STEM focus and Mapua brand.
Listed on Philippine Stock Exchange. Revenue PHP 5.32B FY2024 (~$95M). Public market accountability drives governance discipline.
Large addressable market for tertiary education. Population growth + rising middle class = sustained enrollment demand.

external_indicators.indicator: Board exam pass rates as quality signal
external_indicators.detail: Mapua University is top engineering school. Pass rates are the primary quality benchmark in Philippine HE market.

internal_indicators.value: 2-3.6% net margin
internal_indicators.detail: Thin margins on PHP 5.32B revenue. PHP 351M net income leaves little buffer for investment in technology or expansion.

internal_indicators.value: +10% YoY
internal_indicators.detail: 74,000 students growing at 10% annually. Pipeline from K-12 programs feeds into HE institutions.

internal_indicators.value: Yuchengco + Ayala
internal_indicators.detail: Joint ownership by two large conglomerates. Strategic alignment risk if parent priorities diverge.

external_indicators.detail: Dominant position in UK HE operations software. 350+ institutional clients. Near-saturation means growth must come from ARPU or geography.
180M+ bed-nights/year managed. UK PBSA is a 50B+ GBP asset class with institutional investment driving professionalization and software demand.
Generic PropTech platforms (Yardi, RealPage) could move into education vertical. Kinetic moat is domain expertise but faces platform scale disadvantage.
OfS (Office for Students) increasing scrutiny on student welfare, accommodation standards, and data handling. Creates compliance demand that benefits Kinetic.

external_indicators.indicator: SaaS transition / cloud migration in UK HE
external_indicators.detail: Universities moving from on-prem to cloud. Kinetic SaaS model aligns with institutional procurement trends.

internal_indicators.value: ~86 employees
internal_indicators.detail: Small team for dominant market position. Capacity constraints on product development, support, and international expansion.

internal_indicators.value: Early stage
internal_indicators.detail: 85% UK penetration means growth requires international markets. Different regulatory and market dynamics in each country.

internal_indicators.value: Multi-product (accommodation + conferencing + catering + engagement)
internal_indicators.detail: Cross-sell across product lines within existing customer base. Student engagement module is newest growth vector.

external_indicators.detail: $154M in 2025 projected to $847M by 2034. Provincial markets remain significantly underpenetrated.
PACUCOA Level III is the highest accreditation level. Creates structural barrier for competitors and validates quality positioning.

external_indicators.indicator: Free HE Act / UAQTEA tuition subsidies
external_indicators.detail: Government subsidy covers tuition for students at accredited institutions. Policy reversal would directly impact affordability for PHINMA target market.
PHINMA University of Pangasinan ranked #1 nursing school nationally. 9 of Top 10 passers. Self-reinforcing brand flywheel.
PHINMA Araullo University named Top 1 performing school nationwide for August 2025 Criminology Licensure Exam.
177,851 students across 3,500-5,000 FTE (not all teaching). Bare-bones philosophy suggests lean staffing. Faculty quality critical for board exam results.
KKR Global Impact Fund II. Digital transformation is explicit partnership mandate. Validates growth thesis and funds acquisition pipeline.
High for affordable segment. Quasi-subscription revenue model depends on retention. Each point of retention = ~1,780 students.
Ambitious Indonesia expansion via Horizon University (Karawang) and Kalbis University (Jakarta). JV structure adds complexity.

internal_indicators.value: P6.5B rev / P1.5B NI (~23% margin)
internal_indicators.detail: Strong profitability for education sector. 14% YoY revenue growth. Disciplined cost control.

internal_indicators.value: Early stage
internal_indicators.detail: Proprietary PHINMA Ed Learning App deployed but basic (scheduling, attendance, grades). CIO in place. KKR mandate includes digital transformation.

internal_indicators.value: Structural shortage
internal_indicators.detail: Provincial markets have scarce licensed healthcare educators. Board exam performance depends on faculty quality — critical constraint for growth.

external_indicators.detail: 11-19% CAGR. 250M+ out-of-school children globally, concentrated in SSA. Massive unmet demand.
Series A $4.25M (2024) from impact investors. Total cumulative $6-10M+. Sector attracting serious development capital.
Rori AI tutor delivered via WhatsApp reaches 150,000+ users. Zero app-install friction. WhatsApp is the platform in West Africa.
Sierra Leone, Liberia, Ghana, Rwanda. Government contracts provide scale but add political risk and payment delays.
Low-cost private school chains face political backlash in some African markets. Rising B-Corp / evidence-led positioning differentiates but sector risk exists.
One of few SSA operators with rigorous evidence base. Learning gains validated through randomized controlled trials. Critical for donor/government credibility.

internal_indicators.value: Grant-subsidised, not yet profitable
internal_indicators.detail: Estimated $2-8M total budget. Trending toward earned revenue but still dependent on grants and philanthropic capital.

internal_indicators.value: Rapid expansion
internal_indicators.detail: 300,000+ students. Growth outpacing infrastructure. Quality consistency across 900 sites in 4 countries is the central operational challenge.

internal_indicators.value: Early but differentiated
internal_indicators.detail: WhatsApp-native AI tutoring is a genuine product differentiator. 150,000+ users. Question is whether it can drive earned revenue.

external_indicators.detail: Australian charitable giving is stable but competition for donor dollars is intense. Cost-of-living pressures may reduce discretionary giving.
Annual Gala Ball is the primary fundraising event. Single-event concentration creates significant revenue volatility risk.
Operates 10 schools in rural Uganda. Education policy changes, land tenure, and foreign NGO regulations are ongoing risks.
SFL model provides dramatically better ratios. 4,500 students across 250 staff = ~18:1 total ratio. Strong differentiation from government alternative.
One of the world youngest populations (median age ~16). Sustained demand for education. Question is funding capacity to serve it.
Revenue raised in AUD, costs incurred in UGX. Currency movements directly affect purchasing power and operational budget.

internal_indicators.value: 10 schools, 4,500 students
internal_indicators.detail: Small but deep. Holistic model (primary + secondary + vocational) over 17 years. Growth constrained by fundraising capacity.

internal_indicators.value: 250 Uganda + ~30 Australia
internal_indicators.detail: Management span across two continents with very different operating contexts. Coordination overhead for a small org.

internal_indicators.value: Low — concentrated in events + major donors
internal_indicators.detail: Estimated $2-5M annual. Gala Ball + small number of major donors = moderate concentration risk. Limited recurring/institutional funding.

external_indicators.detail: QF is state-backed, funded by hydrocarbon endowment. Provides operational stability but creates single-funder dependency.
WISE is part of Qatar knowledge economy and soft power strategy. Continued relevance depends on Qatar strategic priorities for global education influence.
UNESCO, World Bank, OECD, ASU+GSV, HundrED all compete for education innovation mindshare. WISE differentiates on policy + Global South focus.
Called Nobel Prize for Education. Key brand asset. 12,000+ cumulative summit attendees. 35,000+ community members.
AI in education is the dominant theme in global education policy. WISE positioned to convene this conversation but needs to lead, not follow.

internal_indicators.value: Qatar Foundation ≈ 100%
internal_indicators.detail: Operational funding entirely from QF. Zero earned revenue diversification. Strategic alignment with QF leadership is existential.

internal_indicators.value: ~36 FTE
internal_indicators.detail: Very small team for global ambition. Limits capacity for year-round programming, research output, and community engagement.

internal_indicators.value: Limited
internal_indicators.detail: Convening impact is hard to measure. ROI narrative to QF depends on soft metrics (attendees, media mentions, community size) rather than hard outcomes.

external_indicators.value: ~1.05% per annum (2024 actuals); projections to 700,000 by 2050
external_indicators.detail: ACT population is growing but at ~1.05% p.a. — slower than the national rate and significantly slower than western Sydney (3-4%). This provides a modest enrolment tailwind but not a structural demand surge. Birth rates are expected to decline; growth is predominantly from net migration, which skews the population older and more transient.

external_indicators.indicator: ACT geographic enrolment divergence — growth zones vs decline zones
external_indicators.value: Molonglo Valley and Gungahlin growing; Tuggeranong declining (12 of 20 government schools projected to shrink by 2035)
external_indicators.detail: CECG's school network spans both high-growth northern corridors (where new families are concentrating) and declining southern suburbs. Network capacity planning is needed to match supply to shifting demand within the ACT footprint.

external_indicators.indicator: CECG total system enrolment trend (18-year ACARA series)
external_indicators.detail: CECG enrolments have grown every year since 2020 after a flat-to-declining period 2009-2019. The 2025 figure of 27,186 represents the highest recorded total and outperforms the underlying ACT population growth rate. The 2025 YoY growth rate of 2.6% is the highest in the 18-year series.

external_indicators.indicator: Independent school sector share growth nationally
external_indicators.detail: The independent sector is growing at 2.7x the Catholic rate nationally. In the ACT specifically, ACT public school enrolments are declining as independents rise. CECG faces structural sector share pressure from premium independent schools (Canberra Grammar, Radford, St Edmund's) which compete for the same secondary-age families.

external_indicators.value: 27 NSW schools, 7,245 students; flat or modest growth (3% in 2024 vs ACT 1%)
external_indicators.detail: NSW enrolments grew 3% in 2024 vs ACT 1%, suggesting NSW schools are holding or slightly outperforming. However, many Goulburn diocese schools are in small rural towns (Adelong, Bombala, Grenfell, Lake Cargelligo) with limited structural population tailwinds. Small school viability is an ongoing watch item.

external_indicators.indicator: Stromlo Forest Anglican College opening (Molonglo Valley)
external_indicators.value: ELC + Kindergarten opens Feb 2026; full K-12 by 2029 (1,850 student capacity)
external_indicators.detail: Stromlo Forest Anglican College is a purpose-built, well-resourced new independent school opening directly in Molonglo Valley — the ACT's fastest-growing corridor and CECG's primary near-term enrolment opportunity zone. It will grow through the year levels in parallel with the suburb's demographic maturation, making it a persistent structural competitor over a 5+ year horizon.

external_indicators.indicator: ACT Government public school expansion program
external_indicators.value: 5 new public schools opened 2022-2025; Aunty Agnes Shea HS (Taylor) opened 2025; P-6 school at Strathnairn (Ginninderry) opens 2026
external_indicators.detail: The ACT Government is actively building public school capacity in the same northern growth corridors where CECG would benefit from enrolment growth. Each new public school captures a portion of the incoming young family cohort before CECG can. ACT already has higher-than-average public school attendance rates vs other states.

external_indicators.indicator: Catholic market share in ACT vs national
external_indicators.value: Catholic sector ACT growth 0.23% (recent data); national Catholic growth 5.7% (2021-2025)
external_indicators.detail: CECG's ACT market share growth is modest compared to national Catholic sector trends and substantially below the independent sector. This is consistent with the ACT's high public school preference and strong independent competitor presence, but it means CECG cannot rely on passive enrolment growth from demographic trends alone.

external_indicators.indicator: Better and Fairer Schools Agreement (BFSA) 2025-2034 commenced
external_indicators.value: Commonwealth commits to 80%+ SRS for non-government schools; 3.1% SRS indexation in 2025; Year 1 phonics check and numeracy check mandated
external_indicators.detail: BFSA reform requirements (phonics check, evidence-based teaching, wellbeing support, teacher retention) are broadly aligned with what CECG's Catalyst programme is already doing. This means CECG is likely compliant from day one at lower cost than dioceses that have not invested in similar frameworks. The 3.1% indexation provides modest funding uplift.

external_indicators.value: Described as 'severe funding reductions' by outgoing ED Ross Fox
external_indicators.detail: Fox's departure statement explicitly cited Gonski reforms as having created severe funding reductions during his tenure, indicating CECG experienced a real funding contraction. The BFSA trajectory appears more stable, but the legacy impact on financial reserves and operating capacity is unquantified (financial statements not extractable from annual report PDF).

external_indicators.value: Catholic schools nationally receive $10.4bn in 2025; 80%+ SRS from Commonwealth; 3.1% indexation
external_indicators.detail: BFSA provides a stable funding floor for the 2025-2034 decade. ACT non-government schools receive Commonwealth and ACT Territory grants (ACT is a territory, not a state, with a different funding formula). Schools previously funded above the target share will be transitioned smoothly to target by 2029.

external_indicators.indicator: ACT public school teacher starting salary
external_indicators.value: $91,396 starting salary by 2026 — highest in Australia
external_indicators.detail: ACT Government enterprise agreement (July 2023) locked in the highest starting teacher salary in Australia by 2026. CECG faces a structural pay disadvantage when competing with the public sector for new teachers and graduates. The 3-3.5% CECG teacher salary increase in 2024 was explicitly cited as a cost driver for the 2026 fee increase, suggesting CECG is at or near its limits of salary-based competition.

external_indicators.value: ACT public schools entered 2025 effectively fully staffed (~25 vacancies across 4,000 teachers)
external_indicators.detail: The ACT public sector has resolved its teacher shortage by offering Australia's highest salaries, reduced hours, and 60% casual loading. This tightens the available teacher pool for CECG — teachers who previously might have moved between sectors are now retained by the public sector. No equivalent CECG staffing data is available for direct comparison.

external_indicators.indicator: AI in schools — regulatory and adoption context
external_indicators.value: AI discussed at CECG 2025 System Day; no specific AI policy or platform investment announced
external_indicators.detail: AI classroom integration is on CECG's agenda (discussed at the 2025 System Day as part of the Catalyst framework), but no specific AI product, policy framework, or procurement decision has been publicly announced. This is a gap signal — CECG has not yet publicly committed to a platform or governance approach for AI in classrooms.

internal_indicators.value: 27,186 total enrolments in 2025 (ACARA); 22,912 systemic enrolments per 2024 annual report census
internal_indicators.detail: ACARA data (including congregational schools) shows 27,186 enrolments in 2025, a 2.6% YoY increase and +10.4% from 2019. Annual report August 2024 census recorded 22,912 systemic students across 56 schools. Both figures confirm enrolment growth is real and accelerating. The gap between ACARA total and annual report systemic count reflects congregational school students counted separately by CECG.

internal_indicators.indicator: Catalyst programme — national and international adoption
internal_indicators.value: CET Tasmania adopted Catalyst model (2022 visit → Insight into Learning programme); NZ delegation 30 leaders May 2025; co-presented at Australian Catholic Education Conference Aug 2025
internal_indicators.detail: CECG is the originator of a nationally recognised explicit instruction model. Catholic Education Tasmania's improved NAPLAN results following Catalyst adoption provide external validation. NZ Catholic delegation in May 2025 indicates potential international licensing or consulting opportunity. CSPD's Uplift programme was also modelled on Catalyst. This positions CECG as a thought leader with potential IP/consulting revenue stream.

internal_indicators.value: Top 4 improved diocese for 9 of 10 NAPLAN domains vs national average 2019-2023; largest positive shift of any large diocese 2019-2022; DIBELS 75%+ K-2 students at low risk of reading difficulty
internal_indicators.detail: NAPLAN data shows CECG achieved the largest positive shift of any large Australian diocese between 2019 and 2022, and remained in the top 4 improved dioceses for 9 of 10 domains in 2023. DIBELS composite shows 75%+ of K-2 students at low risk of future reading difficulties. 13 of top 20 ACARA 'Making a Difference' schools in ACT 2023/24 are CECG schools. These are verified figures from source documents.

internal_indicators.value: 800 temporary teachers being moved to permanency; ~85% converted by December 2024
internal_indicators.detail: CECG's strategic decision to convert contract teachers to permanent staff reduces turnover and supports retention. Staff retention levels over 90% are reported. This directly addresses the workforce instability risk and may be a competitive differentiator for teacher recruitment against the public sector's higher salaries.

internal_indicators.value: 88.6% integrate HITP daily; 92.4% believe it merits continued focus; 97% would recommend HITP to colleagues; 79% would not go back to previous teaching approach
internal_indicators.detail: Teacher engagement surveys at System Day 2025 and Deloitte evaluation show very high commitment to the Catalyst framework. These are unusually high scores for a system-wide change programme. Catalyst may function as a non-salary retention mechanism — teachers report professional satisfaction and time savings of up to 6 hours/week.
internal_indicators.source: web-research.json > catalyst_updates (catalyst.cg.catholic.edu.au/defining-the-journey-for-2025-system-day); annual-report-extract.json > catalyst_impact results (catalyst-deloitte-evaluation.pdf p.7)

internal_indicators.value: Employed casual relief teachers on full-time basis to address shortages, particularly in regional schools
internal_indicators.detail: The 2024 annual report identifies system-wide staffing shortages as a named challenge, addressed through a CRT-as-FTE model. This is a workaround, not a structural fix. Regional schools remain most exposed. The permanency conversion programme partially addresses this but the underlying challenge of competing with ACT public sector salaries remains.

internal_indicators.value: Primary: 5.3-10% increase; secondary: 3.5-7.5%; family maintenance levy capping at $1,000
internal_indicators.detail: Fee increases significantly above CPI have attracted media scrutiny (Region Canberra, neutral-to-mildly critical tone). CECG attributed the increases to ICT costs, insurance, teacher salary increases, and a fee harmonisation programme across primary schools. ED de Carvalho positioned it as '$10 a day per child' for primary. The optics risk is real given ACT families already face high cost of living and have public school and independent school alternatives.

internal_indicators.value: Revenue, expenditure, and government grants data not available (scanned PDF pages)
internal_indicators.detail: Annual report financial statements (pages 34-41) are scanned image pages with no extractable text. No revenue, expenditure, or surplus/deficit figures can be confirmed. This is a data gap, not a signal in itself.

internal_indicators.value: Third year of operation in 2025; 60-100 students annually; 7+ schools; 12 courses offered via Microsoft Teams
internal_indicators.detail: CECG's virtual learning programme solves a real equity and access problem for regional and rural Year 11-12 students who would otherwise leave their communities. Led by an award-winning coordinator (Dr Wendy Mockler, 2025 Rural and Remote Education Advocate Award). Uses Microsoft Teams. This is a scalable workforce efficiency model as well as an equity initiative.

internal_indicators.value: 5,400+ curriculum resources (K-6 English and Mathematics); Knowledge Pack 3.0 released July 2024 (60+ curated items)
internal_indicators.detail: CECG and Ochre Education have co-developed 5,400+ curriculum resources available via an online library. These are reportedly the most-downloaded resources on the Catalyst site. Knowledge Packs (three versions) serve as teacher professional learning collections. Ochre has since partnered with Smartgrade for an impact assessment platform — CECG may benefit from this capability.

internal_indicators.value: ICT cited as a rising cost driver in 2026 fee communications
internal_indicators.detail: CECG explicitly named ICT as a driver of cost increases justifying the 2026 fee rises. This confirms ongoing investment in school-level technology infrastructure but no specific system (SIS, LMS, or assessment platform) is publicly named. The nature and scale of technology investment is not transparent from available sources.

internal_indicators.value: Only Catholic education system in Australia operating across both a state (NSW) and a territory (ACT)
internal_indicators.detail: CECG operates under two different education regulatory frameworks simultaneously — ACT (territory) and NSW (state). This creates compliance complexity, dual reporting requirements, and different funding formula implications. It is also a differentiated capability and signals organisational maturity in managing regulatory complexity.

internal_indicators.value: CECG incorporated as Catholic Archdiocese of Canberra and Goulburn Education Limited from 1 January 2025
internal_indicators.detail: Transition from Catholic Education Commission (established 1983) to a registered not-for-profit company with skills-based board enhances governance transparency and lay participation. This is a significant structural change that modernises accountability and may improve access to capital or commercial partnerships. It occupied substantial Commission attention in 2024.

external_risks.label: Stromlo Forest Anglican College — direct competition in Molonglo Valley growth corridor
external_risks.reasoning: SFAC is a fully funded, purpose-built Anglican school opening in the ACT's highest-growth residential corridor, staged to reach 1,850 students by 2029. Molonglo Valley is precisely where CECG needs new enrolments to grow. CECG has no school in the Molonglo Valley catchment currently. The risk is not just of share loss but of being permanently locked out of a growth corridor by a well-resourced competitor with a 5-year head start on a new student cohort.

external_risks.label: ACT public teacher salary escalation — structural recruitment disadvantage
external_risks.reasoning: ACT public schools will pay the highest starting teacher salary in Australia ($91,396 by 2026). CECG's 3-3.5% salary increases are clearly insufficient to close this gap. While Catalyst and permanency conversion provide non-salary retention levers, the recruitment disadvantage for new graduates entering a tight labour market is real and documented. ACT public sector has already resolved its shortage, meaning fewer teachers in the available pool.

external_risks.label: Independent sector structural share gain — long-term market erosion
external_risks.reasoning: National data shows independent schools growing 2.7x faster than Catholic schools (15.3% vs 5.7%, 2021-2025). In the ACT, ACT public enrolments are declining while independents rise. CECG's own ACT Catholic growth is modest at 0.23%. The structural preference for independent schooling among ACT's higher-income, more secular demographic is a medium-term share erosion risk. Catalyst outcomes may partially counteract this if marketed effectively, but no evidence of that translation yet.

external_risks.label: ACT school-age demographic shift — geographic mismatch with CECG network
external_risks.reasoning: 12 of 20 ACT government schools in Tuggeranong are projected to shrink by 2035, and growth is concentrated in Molonglo Valley and along the light rail corridor to Woden. CECG has schools in declining catchments (southern suburbs) and no presence in Molonglo Valley. Without network expansion in growth zones or rationalisation in decline zones, CECG faces an increasing geographic mismatch between school capacity and enrolment demand.

external_risks.label: Fee increases above CPI — parent affordability pressure and reputational risk
external_risks.reasoning: 2026 fee increases of 5.3-10% (primary) and 3.5-7.5% (secondary) have attracted neutral-to-critical media coverage. Families facing cost-of-living pressure in the ACT may reconsider Catholic schooling when public alternatives are free and well-resourced. The risk is manageable if increases are temporary (harmonisation) but ongoing cost pressures (ICT, insurance, salaries) suggest above-CPI fees are structural, not one-off. ED de Carvalho's affordability messaging is noted but untested.

external_risks.label: Gonski funding trajectory — lingering exposure to future reform
external_risks.reasoning: The BFSA 2025-2034 provides a stable funding framework for the medium term, but Gonski-era funding reductions were described as 'severe' and impacted CECG's operating model under Fox's tenure. A future federal government could revisit non-government school funding arrangements. Score is low given the 10-year agreement, but not zero given the precedent of the Gonski 2.0 reductions.

external_risks.label: Catalyst IP diffusion — competitive advantage erosion as model is adopted widely
external_risks.reasoning: CSPD modelled Uplift on Catalyst; Catholic Education Tasmania launched Insight into Learning after a 2022 CECG visit; NZ Catholic Education is studying the model. As Catalyst diffuses, CECG's differentiation as a thought leader erodes unless it continues to innovate ahead of adopters. The risk is not existential (enrolment outcomes remain internally valid) but the external reputation advantage shrinks over time.

internal_risks.label: Leadership transition — dual turnover at ED level (Fox out, Betts interim, de Carvalho incoming mid-2025)
internal_risks.reasoning: CECG had three different people in the ED role across a 12-month window (Fox resigned Oct 2024, Betts interim from Jan 2025, de Carvalho commencing mid-2025). This is significant instability at the apex of a 56-school system during a period that includes incorporation as CACGEL, above-CPI fee increases, and continued Catalyst expansion. De Carvalho has strong national credentials (ACARA CEO, NESA CEO, NCEC CEO) but has not previously run a diocesan school system. Risk is elevated in the first 12-18 months.

internal_risks.label: Patrick Ellis key person risk — Catalyst programme continuity
internal_risks.reasoning: Patrick Ellis is Education Lead and the named driver of Catalyst's external partnerships (NZ delegation, CET conference presentation). His departure risk (originally flagged as a possible move to CSPD) is not confirmed — he remains at CECG as of August 2025 per web sources. However, his visibility in every major Catalyst external engagement makes him a key person. If Ellis were to leave, the programme's external expansion and internal momentum could be disrupted. Score is moderate because: (1) departure is unconfirmed, (2) Catalyst has broad teacher engagement making it less single-person dependent, and (3) Deloitte evaluation framework provides structural continuity.

internal_risks.label: Financial opacity — inability to verify revenue, expenditure or surplus from published annual report
internal_risks.reasoning: Financial statements in the 2024 annual report are scanned image pages with no extractable text, so no revenue, expenditure, government grant, or surplus/deficit figures are verifiable from the primary source. This is a due-diligence gap. CECG's incorporation as CACGEL from Jan 2025 may improve financial disclosure going forward, but current financial health cannot be independently assessed. The Gonski funding reduction history, above-CPI fee increases, and ICT cost escalation suggest a system under financial pressure, but severity is unquantifiable without data.

internal_risks.label: Regional school viability — access to therapy, staffing, and enrolment fragility
internal_risks.reasoning: The annual report explicitly identifies access to allied health therapy (speech, occupational therapy) in regional schools as a challenge, and staffing shortages as disproportionately affecting regional campuses. Many Goulburn diocese schools are in small rural towns (Adelong, Bombala, Grenfell, Lake Cargelligo, West Wyalong) where the school-age population is flat or declining. Small school viability is a recurring issue across rural Catholic systems in Australia.

internal_risks.label: Governance transition complexity — CACGEL incorporation operational risk
internal_risks.reasoning: Transitioning 56 schools, 1,505 FTE teachers, and all operations into a new corporate structure from 1 January 2025 was described as occupying 'much of the Commission's attention in 2024'. New governance brings benefits (modern accountability, potential commercial partnerships) but also risk: unclear obligations, potential for board-management tension under new structure, and an incoming ED unfamiliar with the new entity. Risk is low because the transition is now complete and CACGEL is operational.

internal_risks.label: Catalyst scale vs depth trade-off — risk of programme dilution as it expands nationally
internal_risks.reasoning: Catalyst is deepening internally (Knowledge Pack 3.0, Deloitte evaluation, Tom Bennett workshop, Wexler co-hosting) while simultaneously expanding externally (NZ, Tasmania, national conference). There is a programme management risk that the external thought leadership activity absorbs capacity that should be sustaining and deepening internal outcomes. 88.6% daily HITP integration (from 2025 System Day) is strong but not 100%, suggesting some schools or teachers are not yet fully embedded. Score is low because the evidence base remains strong.

news_items.headline: Man of faith, humanity, and family to head education in Canberra-Goulburn Archdiocese
news_items.summary: David de Carvalho appointed permanent Executive Director of CECG, commencing mid-2025. Former CEO of ACARA, NESA, and National Catholic Education Commission. Archbishop Prowse welcomed the appointment. De Carvalho has signalled focus on affordability and procurement efficiencies.

news_items.headline: Pam Betts appointed as Interim Executive Director
news_items.summary: Former Executive Director of Catholic Education Brisbane came out of retirement to serve as Interim ED from 6 January 2025 while the Board recruited a permanent replacement for Ross Fox.

news_items.headline: Praise for outgoing Catholic education leader
news_items.summary: Ross Fox resigned after eight years as Executive Director. His tenure saw record enrolments and the launch of Catalyst. Archbishop Prowse praised his navigation of Gonski funding reductions. Catalyst described as an outstanding success.

news_items.headline: Catholic school fees are going up beyond CPI in 2026. Here's why
news_items.summary: Region Canberra reported primary fee increases of 5.3-10% and secondary increases of 3.5-7.5% for 2026. CECG cited ICT costs, insurance, teacher salary increases, and fee harmonisation as drivers. ED de Carvalho offered affordability messaging. Tone was neutral to mildly critical.

news_items.headline: New era in governance of Catholic Education (CACGEL incorporation)
news_items.summary: CECG incorporated as Catholic Archdiocese of Canberra and Goulburn Education Limited (CACGEL) from 1 January 2025, transitioning from the Catholic Education Commission structure established in 1983. New skills-based board with enhanced lay participation and corporate accountability.

news_items.headline: Showcasing Catalyst — Welcoming New Zealand
news_items.summary: Thirty school leaders and teachers from Catholic Education New Zealand visited 10 CECG schools over three days to observe High Impact Teaching Practice (HITP) in action. Education Lead Patrick Ellis led the delegation. Confirms growing international interest in Catalyst as an exportable model. Ellis confirmed still at CECG as of this date.

news_items.headline: A Tale of Two Systems — CECG and Catholic Education Tasmania at Australian Catholic Education Conference
news_items.summary: Patrick Ellis co-presented with Jennifer White (CET) in a session before 200+ delegates at the ACEC in Cairns. CET's Insight into Learning programme (modelled on Catalyst) showed NAPLAN improvement across all domains. CECG confirmed as the national originator of the explicit instruction movement in Catholic education.

news_items.headline: 2025 CECG System Day — over 2,000 educators participate
news_items.summary: System Day featured keynotes from Natalie Wexler and Professor Daniel Willingham. Survey of 1,500+ teachers: 88.6% integrate HITP daily; 92.4% believe it merits continued focus; 83.3% would sustain use beyond formal requirements. Theme was 'Pilgrims of Hope'.

news_items.headline: Stromlo Forest Anglican College to open high school early
news_items.summary: New independent Anglican school in Molonglo Valley (Wright) opening February 2026 with ELC and Kindergarten, growing to K-12 by 2029 with final capacity of 1,850 students. Direct competitor in ACT's highest-growth residential corridor where CECG has no current school.

news_items.headline: Access and equity: Virtual learning program opens up possibilities for regional students
news_items.summary: Dr Wendy Mockler won the 2025 Rural and Remote Education Advocate Award from Professional Teachers' Council NSW for her work coordinating CECG's virtual learning programme. Programme in third year of operation, serving 60-100 students annually across 7+ rural schools via Microsoft Teams.

news_items.headline: Educators urged to place faith before success
news_items.summary: CathNews covered de Carvalho addressing Catholic educators, calling on them to place the Gospel — rather than conventional success metrics — as the guiding principle for their work. Early signal of his strategic priorities and communications style as incoming ED.

news_items.headline: Natalie Wexler workshop co-hosted by CECG (November 2025)
news_items.summary: CECG co-hosted two Natalie Wexler workshops at St Mary MacKillop College Canberra with Think Forward Educators. Sessions covered knowledge gap and writing instruction. Continued external expert investment in literacy and professional learning beyond the annual System Day.
